topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien beneath my fingertips. I traced the elegant script, the dates, the accomplishments—a life meticulously constructed, yet utterly foreign. Every sentence detailed my childhood memories, my supposed triumphs, the very contours of my personality, all presented as established fact. A slow, cold tremor started in my hands, making the edges of the document tremble. I reread the passage about my career trajectory, the words smooth and polished, but they didn't resonate. A tightness constricted my chest, making each breath shallow. I pushed the paper away, the sound a sharp rustle in the quiet study. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I stood up abruptly, needing space, needing to physically distance myself from the fabricated narrative laid bare before me.

[story 2]

It happened during a routine archival search. I was looking for old correspondence, and tucked inside a brittle ledger, I found it: my biography. The prose was flawless, imbued with a reverence that felt deeply misplaced. As my eyes scanned the description of my early struggles, a sudden, hot flush crept up my neck. I leaned closer, my breath catching in my throat. The sheer audacity of it—this complete reshaping of who I am—made my stomach churn violently. I dropped the ledger onto the mahogany desk. The resulting clatter echoed loudly in the room, and I didn't move to pick it up. Instead, I walked to the window, staring out at the rain-slicked cityscape, my shoulders hunching inward as if trying to shield myself from an invisible impact.

[story 3]

When the editor handed me the final draft, the air seemed to thin out. It was my life, meticulously cataloged, every anecdote, every ambition, every quiet moment framed by someone else's perspective. A strange hollowness expanded in my core. I stared at the signature line, the ink seeming to mock me. My gaze drifted down to my own hands, noticing the slight tremor that had taken hold. I picked up the document, the weight of it suddenly immense, suffocating. I didn't speak immediately. I simply held it, my knuckles turning white against the paper, the silence between us stretching taut and unbearable until I finally let the manuscript slip from my grasp, letting it fall onto the thick rug as if it were contaminated.
